stem: A coffee roaster wants to price a specialty blend so that the profit is exactly 30% of the production cost. If the production cost is $18 per pound and the roaster must also pay a $2 per pound packaging fee (which is not part of the profit calculation base), what should the selling price be per pound?
explanation: The profit must be 30% of the production cost: profit = 0.30 × 18 = 5.40. The selling price must cover the production cost ($18), the packaging fee ($2), and the profit ($5.40). Therefore, selling price = 18 + 2 + 5.40 = 25.40. The selling price should be $25.40 per pound.

distractorRationale.A: Calculated profit as 30% of production cost only: 18 × 0.30 = 5.40, then added only this to production cost: 18 + 5.40 = 23.40.
distractorRationale.B: Correct: profit is 30% of production cost = 0.30 × 18 = 5.40, selling price = 18 + 2 + 5.40 = 25.40.
distractorRationale.C: Calculated profit as 30% of total cost including packaging: 0.30 × (18 + 2) = 6, then selling price = 18 + 2 + 6 = 26.00.
distractorRationale.D: Calculated profit as 30% of selling price, setting up s = 18 + 2 + 0.30s, solving 0.70s = 20, getting s = 200/7 ≈ 28.57, then incorrectly used 18 + 2 + 0.30 × 28 = 28.40.
